title: When You Should See a Cardiologist About Your Rage
dek: Your heart is not a metaphor. It is a muscle that beats against your ribs, pumping blood and fury through your veins. Sometimes, the rage you feel is not just an emotion—it is a physiological event that demands medical attention.
bodyMarkdown:
Your heart is not a metaphor. It is a muscle that beats against your ribs, pumping blood and fury through your veins. Sometimes, the rage you feel is not just an emotion—it is a physiological event that demands medical attention.

You wake up at 3:14 AM. The digital clock glows red in the dark, but you are not looking at it. You are looking at the ceiling, feeling the heavy, rhythmic thud in your chest. It is not a flutter. It is a hammer. Your jaw is clenched so tight that the masseter muscle aches, a dull throb that radiates up into your temples. Your breath is shallow, catching somewhere near your collarbone. You are furious. You are furious about the email, about the conversation, about the way the world is, about the way you were spoken to. But more than that, your body is furious. Your body is holding the rage, and your heart is doing the heavy lifting.

We are taught that anger is a feeling, a fleeting state of mind that we should breathe through or journal about. But anger is biology. It is a cascade of cortisol and adrenaline, a sudden narrowing of blood vessels, a spike in blood pressure. When you are angry, your body prepares for war. And your heart is the general on the front lines.

Gabor Maté writes extensively about the connection between our emotional lives and our physical health. He notes that when we suppress our emotions, particularly anger, our bodies bear the brunt of that suppression. The stress response, designed to save us from immediate danger, becomes a chronic state. Your heart, which is meant to beat faster only when you are running from a predator, is now beating faster because you are sitting in traffic, or because you are remembering a slight from ten years ago. Maté's work reminds us that the body does not distinguish between physical danger and emotional threat. The physiological response is the same. The wear and tear on your cardiovascular system is the same.

You might notice it in the middle of the day. You are sitting at your desk, and suddenly, your chest feels tight. Not the tightness of anxiety, but a heavy, pressing sensation. Your left arm might feel a little numb, or your neck might ache. You tell yourself it is just stress. You tell yourself you just need to relax. But your body is sending you a message. The rage you have been carrying, the fury you have been swallowing, is taking a toll on your heart.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his groundbreaking work on trauma, explains that the body keeps the score. The trauma we experience, the anger we are not allowed to express, gets stored in our tissues, our muscles, our organs. The heart, with its dense network of nerves and its constant, rhythmic work, is particularly vulnerable to this stored emotion. Van der Kolk's research shows that chronic emotional stress can lead to physical changes in the body, including changes in heart rate variability and an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. Your rage is not just in your head. It is in your heart.

So, when should you see a cardiologist? You should see a cardiologist when your rage feels less like an emotion and more like a physical symptom. You should see a cardiologist when your heart races for no apparent reason, when you feel a heavy pressure in your chest, when your anger is accompanied by shortness of breath or dizziness. You should see a cardiologist when you realize that your body is fighting a battle that your mind cannot win alone.

It is not a failure to seek medical help. It is an act of profound self-care. It is an acknowledgment that your body is real, that your heart is a physical organ that needs attention and care. It is a recognition that the rage you carry is heavy, and that you do not have to carry it alone.

In the meantime, there are things you can do to support your heart. Herbs like [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) have been used for centuries to support cardiovascular health and calm the nervous system. A simple tea of hawthorn berries, drank slowly in the quiet of the morning, can be a gentle way to nourish your heart. But herbs are not a substitute for medical care. They are allies, not saviors.

**Embodied Practice: The Heart Hold**

Find a quiet place to sit or lie down. Place your right hand over your heart, feeling the warmth of your palm against your chest. Place your left hand over your belly. Close your eyes and take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, feeling your belly expand. Exhale slowly through your mouth. As you breathe, focus your attention on the space beneath your right hand. Feel the rhythm of your heart. Acknowledge the work it does for you every single day. Say to yourself, silently or aloud: "I feel you. I hear you. I am taking care of you." Do this for five minutes, allowing yourself to simply be with your heart, without judgment or expectation.

**FAQ**

**Q:** Is it normal for my heart to race when I'm angry?
**A:** Yes, it is a normal physiological response to anger. Adrenaline and cortisol are released, causing your heart rate and blood pressure to increase. However, if your heart races frequently, or if it takes a long time to calm down, it is worth discussing with a doctor.

**Q:** Can anger really cause a heart attack?
**A:** Chronic, unmanaged anger can increase your risk of cardiovascular disease, which can lead to a heart attack. The constant surge of stress hormones can damage your blood vessels and put strain on your heart.

**Q:** I feel silly going to a doctor for anger. What should I say?
**A:** You do not have to say you are there for anger. You can say you are experiencing physical symptoms like a racing heart, chest tightness, or shortness of breath, and you want to make sure your heart is healthy. Be honest about your stress levels, but focus on the physical sensations you are experiencing.

**Q:** Are there herbs that can help with the physical symptoms of anger?
**A:** Yes, herbs like hawthorn, motherwort, and [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support the nervous system and cardiovascular health. However, they should be used in conjunction with, not as a replacement for, medical advice, especially if you are experiencing concerning physical symptoms.



The physical reality of your anger is not something to be ashamed of. It is a testament to your body's incredible capacity to feel, to respond, to protect you. When the trapezius muscles knot up, when the levator scapulae pulls your shoulders toward your ears, your body is bracing for impact. It is preparing to fight a threat that may not be physical, but is very real to your nervous system. 

Consider the diaphragm, the large muscle that sits at the base of your chest. When you are angry, your breathing becomes shallow. You breathe into your upper chest, ignoring the deep, expansive capacity of your lungs. This shallow breathing signals to your brain that you are in danger, perpetuating the cycle of stress and anger. By consciously deepening your breath, by allowing your diaphragm to fully expand and contract, you can begin to signal safety to your nervous system. But this takes practice. It takes a willingness to be present with the discomfort of your anger, rather than running from it or suppressing it.

Your heart, meanwhile, continues its steady, relentless work. It pumps blood to your extremities, preparing you to run or fight. It works harder, faster, demanding more oxygen, more energy. This is why chronic anger is so exhausting. Your body is constantly running a marathon that never ends. It is no wonder that you feel depleted, that your muscles ache, that your sleep is disturbed.

We must also consider the role of the vagus nerve, the wandering nerve that connects your brain to your heart, lungs, and digestive tract. The vagus nerve is a key player in the parasympathetic nervous system, the "rest and digest" system that counterbalances the "fight or flight" response. When you are chronically angry, your vagal tone—the strength and flexibility of your vagus nerve—can become compromised. This makes it harder for your body to return to a state of calm after a stressful event. Practices like deep breathing, humming, and cold exposure can help stimulate the vagus nerve and improve vagal tone, supporting your body's ability to regulate its response to anger.

But again, these practices are not a substitute for medical care. If your heart is struggling, if you are experiencing physical symptoms that concern you, you must seek professional help. A cardiologist can assess the health of your heart, check for any underlying conditions, and provide guidance on how to protect your cardiovascular system. They can help you understand the physical impact of your anger and work with you to develop a plan for managing it.

Do not wait until it is too late. Do not ignore the messages your body is sending you. Your anger is a messenger, yes, but it is also a physical force that can cause real damage if left unchecked. Listen to your heart. Listen to the heavy, rhythmic thud in your chest. It is telling you something important. It is telling you that it needs your attention, your care, your compassion.

You are not broken because you feel angry. You are not flawed because your body responds to stress. You are human, and your body is doing exactly what it was designed to do. But you have the power to support your body, to care for your heart, to seek the help you need. You have the power to transform your relationship with your anger, to move from a place of fear and suppression to a place of understanding and integration.

This journey is not easy. It requires courage, vulnerability, and a willingness to face the darker parts of yourself. It requires you to be honest about your pain, your frustration, your rage. But it is a journey worth taking. Because on the other side of that anger is a deeper connection to yourself, a greater sense of peace, and a healthier, more resilient heart.

So, the next time you wake up at 3:14 AM with your heart pounding in your chest, do not ignore it. Do not tell yourself it is just stress. Place your hand over your heart, feel its rhythm, and acknowledge its work. And then, when the sun comes up, make the call. Schedule the appointment. Take the first step toward caring for your physical heart, so that it can continue to support you in all the ways you need.
